Looking at that rlwimi instruction more carefully: `rlwimi r0, r3, 4, 27, 27` inserts bit 27 of (r3 rotated left by 4) into r0 at bit position 27. Since r3 = 0, rotating it doesn't change anything, so this effectively clears bit 27.

Now I need to figure out which actual bit in the byte at 0xDCA this corresponds to. In PPC's bit numbering where bit 0 is the MSB and bit 31 is the LSB, bit 27 is the 5th bit from the right.

The rlwimi instruction is clearing bit 27 by inserting a 0 there, so this is effectively clearing bit 4 of the byte at offset 0xDCA. Looking at the function signature, the first argument is an Item_GObj pointer and the second is a Fighter_GObj pointer, so I need to check what bitfield in the item structure corresponds to this offset and bit position. I'm working through the bitfield clearing logic—the rlwimi instruction rotates the zero value left by 4 bits and masks it into bit 27, effectively clearing that bit in the byte at offset 0xDCA. So it inserts 0 into bit 27 of r0.

In the byte at 0xDCA, bit 27 of the 32-bit register (with the byte in the low 8 bits after lbz) corresponds to... lbz loads the byte into the low 8 bits of the register (bits 24-31). Bit 27 is within that byte, at position 27-24=3 from the MSB of the byte, or equivalently bit 4 from the LSB of the byte.

So this clears bit 4 (counting from 0 at LSB) of the byte, or equivalently bit 3 counting from 0 at MSB within the byte.

In the CLAUDE.md it says: "m2c '&~1' on a byte means clear LSB. In PPC bitfield order, LSB = x_b7."

So the bits are labeled x_b0 through x_b7, where b0 is MSB and b7 is LSB. Bit 27 in PPC 32-bit corresponds to x_b3 within the byte. Let me verify this mapping: when lbz loads a byte into bits 24-31 of the register, byte bit 0 (MSB) maps to register bit 24, and each subsequent byte bit maps to the next register bit, so byte bit 3 lands on register bit 27.
